text: The surgeon performs urethrolysis and opens the pararectal space to free the rectum from nodular attachments along the posterior cervix and vagina. The retroperitoneum is opened below the infundibulopelvic ligament, revealing the common iliac artery bifurcating into the external and internal iliac arteries. The ureter is identified crossing the common iliac and is lifted and dissected away from its attachment to the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon mobilizes the ureter along its length, lateralizing it away from the nodular attachment. A window is created under the ureter, which is extended using blunt traction, ensuring the ureter is safely lateralized away from the uterus and vagina.

The surgeon performs further lateralization of the ureter, mobilizing it to the level of the ureteric tunnel or corneal space. The retroperitoneum is lifted, allowing the rectum and pararectal space to be mobilized medially. The ureter is gently dissected laterally, freeing it from lateral attachments. The Obakayashi space is opened, revealing the rectum plastered to the posterior cervix. The surgeon dissects the rectum away from the nodule below its level.

The surgeon uses blunt traction to dissect the rectum away from its nodular attachment in the deep pararectal space. Once separated cranially and caudally, gentle coagulation current is applied to further detach the rectum from the nodule. The rectal musculature is skinned, leaving the circular muscles intact, allowing the rectum to be mobilized away from the posterior vaginal wall up to the anorectal junction.
